items.headline: U.S. Stocks Decline Sharply Amid Rising Oil Prices, Geopolitical Tensions, and Tech Earnings Concerns
items.summary: U.S. equities experienced a significant sell-off on Friday, July 24, 2026, with major indices closing broadly lower. The market's downturn was primarily driven by Brent crude prices topping $100 per barrel, escalating geopolitical risks in the Middle East, ongoing concerns about a potential bubble in artificial intelligence investments, and negative reactions to recent earnings reports from several technology companies. Investor sentiment, as measured by the American Association of Individual Investors (AAII), fell to its lowest point since last September, reflecting increased caution among retail investors. Not financial advice.

items.headline: Lockheed Martin Rises on Divergent Valuation
items.summary: Lockheed Martin ($LMT) closed up 10.5% at $568.59. This gain reflects divergent investor expectations, with the company trading at 27.3 times trailing earnings and showing 4.6% revenue growth over the past twelve months.

items.headline: Safety Insurance Soars on Acquisition News
items.summary: Safety Insurance ($SAFT) shares soared 42% after the insurer agreed to be acquired by Mapfre in an all-cash deal valued at approximately $1.54 billion. Shareholders are set to receive $105 per share, a 44% premium to the prior closing price.

items.headline: Amkor Technology Jumps on NVIDIA AI Partnership
items.summary: Amkor Technology ($AMKR) shares jumped 9% after announcing a multi-year strategic partnership with Nvidia ($NVDA) to develop advanced semiconductor packaging and testing technologies for next-generation AI platforms. Nvidia's advance payments will boost Amkor's Arizona packaging capacity.

items.headline: Tesla Plunges Following Earnings Report
items.summary: Tesla ($TSLA) experienced the session's most dramatic decline, falling 14.5%. This sharp drop followed a negative market reaction to its Q2 earnings report, exacerbated by its high valuation (272.0 times trailing earnings) despite 11.8% revenue growth over the past year.

items.headline: Leslies Continues Steep Decline
items.summary: Leslies ($LESL) closed down 44.8%, extending a multi-week trend of weakness. The company's stock also features on the one-month losers list with an 86.5% total return decline, driven by a 7.5% decrease in revenue over the last twelve months.

items.headline: Sector Performance Diverges Amid Broad Market Downturn
items.summary: Despite overall market declines, certain sectors exhibited strength. Rail Transportation and Pharmaceuticals showed notable resilience, contributing to 17 S&P 500 components hitting 52-week highs, including JPMorgan Chase ($JPM). Industrials and Real Estate also posted strong gains, fueled by increased AI data center construction and demand. Conversely, the Energy sector lagged, recording negative returns earlier in the quarter, even as oil prices surged today. Technology remains a key driver, yet financials, industrials, and healthcare are demonstrating improving relative strength.

items.headline: Key Economic Data Released; Fed Reaffirms Inflation Target
items.summary: Today's economic calendar featured the release of Global Flash PMIs for July, Japanese Inflation for June, German GfK Consumer Confidence for August, UK Retail Sales for June, and Canadian PPI for June. The U.S. Treasury Department's semi-annual foreign exchange report noted the yen is 'significantly undervalued' and expressed concern over 'excessive fluctuations' in its exchange rate. Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh reiterated the central bank's commitment to its 2% annual inflation target, despite the PCE index showing inflation above this level for 63 consecutive months. The Fed has maintained the federal funds rate target range at 3.5% to 3.75% since the start of the year. The European Central Bank (ECB) held its main interest rates steady.

items.headline: Major Earnings Reports Drive Individual Stock Movements
items.summary: Q2 earnings reports continued to shape market activity. Intel ($INTC) saw its shares climb 3% after exceeding Q2 revenue and EPS estimates, despite an $11 billion GAAP loss. American Express ($AXP), Verizon ($VZ), NextEra Energy ($NEE), HCA Healthcare ($HCA), and Charter Communications ($CHTR) were among the major companies reporting Q2 results before the market open. However, negative reactions to Q2 earnings from Tesla ($TSLA) and Alphabet ($GOOGL) contributed to the broader market's decline. Nasdaq, Inc. ($NDAQ) reported a 15% increase in Q2 net revenue and a 14% rise in GAAP diluted EPS, while Ovintiv ($OVV) posted Q2 net earnings of $456 million.
